subtopics.name: Microscopy in cell studies
learning_outcomes: make temporary preparations of cellular material suitable for viewing with a light microscope [cite: 8]
draw cells from microscope slides and photomicrographs [cite: 8]
calculate magnifications of images and actual sizes of specimens from drawings, photomicrographs and electron micrographs (scanning and transmission) [cite: 8]
use an eyepiece graticule and stage micrometer scale to make measurements and use the appropriate units, millimetre (mm), micrometre (µm) and nanometre (nm) [cite: 8]
define resolution and magnification and explain the differences between these terms, with reference to light microscopy and electron microscopy [cite: 8]

subtopics.name: Eukaryotic and prokaryotic cell structures
learning_outcomes: recognise organelles and other cell structures found in eukaryotic cells and outline their structures and functions [cite: 8]
describe and interpret photomicrographs, electron micrographs and drawings of typical plant and animal cells [cite: 17, 18]
compare the structure of typical plant and animal cells [cite: 19]
state that cells use ATP from respiration for energy-requiring processes [cite: 20]
outline key structural features of a prokaryotic cell as found in a typical bacterium [cite: 21, 22]
compare the structure of a prokaryotic cell as found in a typical bacterium with the structures of typical eukaryotic cells in plants and animals [cite: 29]
state that all viruses are non-cellular structures with a nucleic acid core and a capsid made of protein [cite: 30]

subtopics.name: Testing for biological molecules
learning_outcomes: describe and carry out the Benedict's test for reducing sugars, the iodine test for starch, the emulsion test for lipids and the biuret test for proteins [cite: 44]
describe and carry out a semi-quantitative Benedict's test on a reducing sugar solution [cite: 44]
describe and carry out a test to identify the presence of non-reducing sugars, using acid hydrolysis and Benedict's solution [cite: 44]

subtopics.name: Carbohydrates and lipids structure
learning_outcomes: describe and draw the ring forms of a-glucose and ß-glucose [cite: 44]
define the terms monomer, polymer, macromolecule, monosaccharide, disaccharide and polysaccharide [cite: 44]
state the role of covalent bonds in joining smaller molecules together to form polymers [cite: 44]
state that glucose, fructose and maltose are reducing sugars and that sucrose is a non-reducing sugar [cite: 44]
describe the formation of a glycosidic bond by condensation [cite: 44]
describe the breakage of a glycosidic bond in polysaccharides and disaccharides by hydrolysis [cite: 54]
describe the molecular structure of starch and glycogen and relate their structures to functions [cite: 55]
describe the molecular structure of cellulose and its role in plant cell walls [cite: 56]
state that triglycerides are non-polar hydrophobic molecules and describe their molecular structure [cite: 57]
relate the molecular structure of triglycerides to their functions in living organisms [cite: 58]
describe the molecular structure of phospholipids with reference to hydrophilic heads and hydrophobic tails [cite: 59]

subtopics.name: Protein structure and function
learning_outcomes: describe and draw the general structure of an amino acid and the formation and breakage of a peptide bond [cite: 62]
explain the meaning of the terms primary structure, secondary structure, tertiary structure and quaternary structure of proteins [cite: 63, 64]
describe the types of interaction that hold protein molecules in shape [cite: 65]
state that globular proteins are generally soluble and fibrous proteins are generally insoluble [cite: 70]
describe the structure of a molecule of haemoglobin as an example of a globular protein [cite: 71]
relate the structure of haemoglobin to its function [cite: 72]
describe the structure of a molecule of collagen as an example of a fibrous protein [cite: 73, 74]
relate the structures of collagen molecules and collagen fibres to their function [cite: 75]

subtopics.name: Water properties and roles
learning_outcomes: explain how hydrogen bonding occurs between water molecules and relate properties to solvent action, high specific heat capacity and latent heat of vaporisation [cite: 83]

subtopics.name: Enzyme mode of action
learning_outcomes: state that enzymes are globular proteins that catalyse reactions inside or outside cells [cite: 91]
explain the mode of action of enzymes in terms of an active site, enzyme-substrate complex, lowering of activation energy and specificity [cite: 91]
investigate the progress of enzyme-catalysed reactions by measuring rates [cite: 91]
outline the use of a colorimeter for measuring the progress of enzyme-catalysed reactions [cite: 91]

subtopics.name: Factors affecting enzyme action
learning_outcomes: investigate and explain the effects of temperature, pH, enzyme, substrate, and inhibitor concentration on enzyme-catalysed reactions [cite: 91]
explain that the maximum rate of reaction (Vmax) is used to derive the Michaelis-Menten constant (Km) [cite: 91]
explain the effects of reversible inhibitors, both competitive and non-competitive, on enzyme activity [cite: 91]
investigate the difference in activity between an immobilised enzyme and the same enzyme free in solution [cite: 91]

subtopics.name: Fluid mosaic membrane model
learning_outcomes: describe the fluid mosaic model of membrane structure [cite: 103]
describe the arrangement of cholesterol, glycolipids and glycoproteins in cell surface membranes [cite: 103]
describe the roles of phospholipids, cholesterol, glycolipids, proteins and glycoproteins in cell surface membranes [cite: 103]
outline the main stages in the process of cell signalling leading to specific responses [cite: 103]

subtopics.name: Movement into and out of cells
learning_outcomes: describe and explain the processes of simple diffusion, facilitated diffusion, osmosis, active transport, endocytosis and exocytosis [cite: 103]
investigate simple diffusion and osmosis using plant tissue and non-living materials [cite: 103]
illustrate the principle that surface area to volume ratios decrease with increasing size [cite: 103]
investigate the effect of changing surface area to volume ratio on diffusion [cite: 103]
investigate the effects of immersing plant tissues in solutions of different water potentials [cite: 112]
explain the movement of water between cells and solutions in terms of water potential [cite: 113]

subtopics.name: Replication and division mechanics
learning_outcomes: describe the structure of a chromosome [cite: 122]
explain the importance of mitosis in the production of genetically identical daughter cells [cite: 122]
outline the mitotic cell cycle including interphase, mitosis and cytokinesis [cite: 122]
outline the role of telomeres in preventing the loss of genes [cite: 122]
outline the role of stem cells in cell replacement and tissue repair [cite: 122]
explain how uncontrolled cell division can result in the formation of a tumour [cite: 122]

subtopics.name: Chromosome behaviour in mitosis
learning_outcomes: describe the behaviour of chromosomes, nuclear envelope, cell surface membrane and spindle during prophase, metaphase, anaphase and telophase [cite: 122]
interpret photomicrographs, diagrams and microscope slides of cells in different stages of the mitotic cell cycle [cite: 122]

subtopics.name: Structure and replication of DNA
learning_outcomes: describe the structure of nucleotides, including ATP [cite: 133]
state that adenine and guanine are purines and cytosine, thymine and uracil are pyrimidines [cite: 133]
describe the structure of a DNA molecule as a double helix [cite: 133]
describe the semi-conservative replication of DNA during the S phase [cite: 133]
describe the structure of an RNA molecule using mRNA as an example [cite: 133]

subtopics.name: Protein synthesis and mutations
learning_outcomes: state that a polypeptide is coded for by a gene [cite: 133]
describe the principle of the universal genetic code [cite: 133]
describe how information in DNA is used during transcription and translation [cite: 141]
state the template vs non-transcribed strands of DNA [cite: 148]
explain the removal of introns and joining of exons to form mRNA in eukaryotes [cite: 149]
state that a gene mutation is a change in base pairs and may alter a polypeptide [cite: 150]
explain gene mutation types: substitution, deletion or insertion [cite: 151, 152]

subtopics.name: Structure of plant transport tissues
learning_outcomes: draw plan diagrams of transverse sections of stems, roots and leaves [cite: 160]
describe the distribution of xylem and phloem in herbaceous dicotyledonous plants [cite: 160]
draw and label xylem vessel elements, phloem sieve tube elements and companion cells [cite: 160]
relate the structure of xylem and phloem elements to their functions [cite: 160]

subtopics.name: Plant transport mechanisms
learning_outcomes: state that some mineral ions and organic compounds can be transported dissolved in water [cite: 160]
describe the transport of water from soil to xylem through apoplast and symplast pathways [cite: 160]
explain that transpiration involves evaporation and diffusion of water vapour [cite: 160]
explain how hydrogen bonding of water is involved with cohesion-tension and adhesion [cite: 160]
make annotated drawings of xerophytic leaves explaining adaptations to reduce water loss [cite: 160]
state that assimilates move from sources to sinks in phloem sieve tubes [cite: 160]
explain how companion cells transfer assimilates to phloem sieve tubes [cite: 160]
explain mass flow in phloem sieve tubes down a hydrostatic pressure gradient [cite: 160]

subtopics.name: The mammalian circulatory system
learning_outcomes: state that the mammalian system is a closed double circulation [cite: 169]
describe functions of pulmonary and systemic blood vessels [cite: 169]
recognise and make plan diagrams of arteries, veins and capillaries [cite: 169]
explain how blood vessel structures are related to functions [cite: 169]
recognise and draw red blood cells, monocytes, neutrophils and lymphocytes [cite: 169]
relate properties of water to its role in mammalian transport [cite: 169]
state functions and formation of tissue fluid [cite: 169]

learning_outcomes: describe the role of red blood cells in transporting oxygen and carbon dioxide [cite: 173]
describe the chloride shift and explain its importance [cite: 173]
describe the role of plasma in carbon dioxide transport [cite: 173]
describe and explain the oxygen dissociation curve of adult haemoglobin [cite: 173]
explain the importance of the oxygen dissociation curve at different partial pressures [cite: 173]
describe the Bohr shift and explain its importance [cite: 173]

subtopics.name: Structure and function of the heart

explain differences in thickness of heart chamber walls [cite: 173]
describe the cardiac cycle with reference to pressure changes and valves [cite: 173]
explain the roles of the sinoatrial node, atrioventricular node and Purkyne tissue [cite: 173]

recognise gas exchange tissues and structures in micrographs and drawings [cite: 181]

subtopics.name: Causative agents and transmission
learning_outcomes: state that infectious diseases are caused by pathogens and are transmissible [cite: 193]
state the name and type of pathogen for cholera, malaria, TB and HIV/AIDS [cite: 193]
explain how cholera, malaria, TB and HIV are transmitted [cite: 193]
discuss biological, social and economic factors in prevention and control [cite: 193]

subtopics.name: Antibiotics and resistance
learning_outcomes: outline how penicillin acts on bacteria and why antibiotics do not affect viruses [cite: 193]
discuss the consequences of antibiotic resistance and reduction steps [cite: 193]

subtopics.name: Mechanisms of the immune system
learning_outcomes: describe the mode of action of phagocytes [cite: 200]
explain antigens and the difference between self and non-self [cite: 200]
describe events during a primary immune response including macrophage and lymphocyte roles [cite: 200]

subtopics.name: Antibodies and vaccination
learning_outcomes: relate the molecular structure of antibodies to their functions [cite: 200]

outline principles of using monoclonal antibodies in diagnosis and treatment [cite: 200]
describe differences between active/passive and natural/artificial immunity [cite: 200]
